text: This is a video demonstration of a medial to lateral approach to the laparoscopic low interior resection. A twenty nine year old female with bleeding underwent colonoscopy which demonstrated a four centimeter mid to low rectal mass with biopsy confirming an adenocarcinoma. MRI showed a T three and one lesion with a close CRM. Staging CT did not show any metastatic disease. After discussion at rectal tumor board she received new adjuvant chemotherapy deferring radiation for fertility preservation. She was subsequently advised to proceed with laparoscopic low anterior resection.
Listed here are the critical steps of the procedure. We will go through each one of these separately throughout this video.

The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum and scores the peritoneum beneath the proximal inferior mesenteric vein, entering the bloodless embryonic fusion plane to separate the mesocolon from the retroperitoneum while protecting contained structures.
The surgeon dissects laterally over Dorota's fascia towards the abdominal sidewall, then focuses on the skeletonization of the inferior mesenteric vein at its origin. The vein is clipped and divided.

The surgeon enters the lesser sac adjacent to the ligament of Treitz and divides the tissues along the inferior border of the pancreas in a medial-lateral direction, facilitating the descent of the pancreas.
A surgical instrument, likely a retractor, is placed underneath the mesocolon, extending towards the splenic flexure, preparing for further dissection in this area.
The surgeon scores the peritoneum beneath the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and develops the plane between the mesocolon and the retroperitoneum, proceeding in a medial to lateral direction.

The surgeon carefully identifies and protects the ureter while lifting the anterior peritoneum from the previous dissection. The peritoneum is scored to connect the dissection of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) to the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), ensuring the ureter remains preserved.

The surgeon skeletonizes the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and divides it between clips using bipolar energy. Tension is released on the mesocolon to prevent bleeding, and the IMA stump is visually assessed for any signs of bleeding.

The surgeon completes the medial dissection and proceeds to divide the lateral attachments of the peritoneum to the sidewall along the white line using monopolar and bipolar energy. The previously placed ratec in the splenic flexure is now visible, confirming the correct dissection plane.

The surgeon opens the lesser sac along and below the gastroepiploic arcade, dividing the omentum towards the splenic flexure. The colon is released up to the middle colic vessels, and the last attachments of the splenic flexure are divided. The total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection continues into the holy plane at the upper rectum, carefully avoiding the hypogastric nerves and proceeding to the pelvic floor.

The surgeon performs a distal transection using a stapler below the tumor level, confirmed by ICG for microvascular perfusion. An end-to-end anastomosis is completed with a 28mm EEA stapler under direct visualization, followed by a negative endoscopic leak test.
The patient's recovery was uneventful and she was discharged home on the third post-operative day. The final pathology of the resected tumor was YP T two N zero M zero with clear margins.
